name: Suspicious Svchost Execution Anomaly
notes:
This rule detects the svchost.exe executed without any CLI arguments which is normally observed when a malicious process spawns the process and injects code into the process memory space.

Covered from SIGMA Rule.

Original Author: David Burkett, @signalblur
SIGMA Title: Suspect Svchost Activity
SIGMA Description: It is extremely abnormal for svchost.exe to spawn without any CLI arguments and is normally observed when a malicious process spawns the process and injects code into the process memory space.

Confidence: MEDIUM

This rule may be covered under the Detection Rule License: https://ibm.biz/SigmaDetectionRuleLicense
conditions_text: when the event(s) were detected by one or more of Microsoft Windows Security Event Log
when the event category for the event is one of the following System.Process Creation Success
when the event matches LOWER("Process Full Full Name") == 'svchost.exe' AQL filter query
when the event matches Command (custom) matches any of expressions svchost\.exe$
when the event matches LOWER("Parent Process Full Full Name") MATCHES '(rpcnet|rpcnetp)\.exe' AQL filter query
TD_list: APPLY Suspicious Svchost Execution Anomaly on events which are detected by the LOCAL system
AND when the event(s) were detected by one or more of Microsoft Windows Security Event Log
AND when the event category for the event is one of the following System.Process Creation Success
AND when the event matches LOWER("Process Full Full Name") == 'svchost.exe' AQL filter query
AND when the event matches Command (custom) matches any of expressions svchost\.exe$
AND NOT when the event matches LOWER("Parent Process Full Full Name") MATCHES '(rpcnet|rpcnetp)\.exe' AQL filter query
RED_list: Event description: Detects the svchost.exe executed without any CLI arguments which is normally observed when a malicious process spawns the process and injects code into the process memory space
Force the dispatched event to create a NEW offense, select the offense using Source IP
This information should contribute to the name of the associated offense(s)
